payload.user_message: what skills do you have access to?

tools.name: bash
tools.description:
Execute bash commands in a persistent session.
Rules:
- Prefer ripgrep (rg) for search.
- Keep outputs small using head, tail, or context flags (-A / -C).
- Avoid full-file dumps and recursive/unbounded scans.
- Never run full-disk scans like `find / ...`.
- Ignore irrelevant directories unless asked: node_modules, __pycache__, .git, venv, dist, build, migrations, tests.
- Truncate outputs to ~50 lines or ~4000 chars.
Examples:
- rg -l "payment" --type py
- rg "def process_payment" -A 12
- tree -L 2 -I 'node_modules|__pycache__|.git'
- sed -n '1,120p' src/app.py

tools.name: Skill
tools.description:
Execute a skill within the main conversation.

<skills_instructions>
When users ask you to perform tasks, check if any of the available skills below can help complete the task more effectively. Skills provide specialized capabilities and domain knowledge.

How to use skills:
- Invoke skills using this tool with the skill name only (no arguments)
- When you invoke a skill, you will see <command-message>The "{name}" skill is loading</command-message>
- The skill's prompt will expand and provide detailed instructions on how to complete the task
</skills_instructions>

<available_skills>
- name: ai-evals
  description: Help users create and run AI evaluations. Use when someone is building evals for LLM products, measuring model quality, creating test cases, designing rubrics, or trying to systematically measure AI output quality.
- name: behavioral-product-design
  description: Help users apply behavioral science to product design. Use when someone is designing for habit formation, reducing friction, applying psychology to UX, increasing retention through behavioral principles, or using nudges to influence user behavior.
- name: founder-sales
  description: Help founders close their first customers and build repeatable sales processes. Use when someone is doing founder-led sales, trying to get their first customers, writing cold outreach, running early sales calls, or asking when to hire their first salesperson.
- name: product-led-sales
  description: Help users implement product-led sales motions. Use when someone is transitioning from pure PLG to sales-assisted, defining PQLs, building sales handoff processes, or trying to expand self-serve users into enterprise contracts.
</available_skills>

tools.name: get_preferences
tools.description: Get stored user preferences for this session. Preferences are persistent across conversations. Check this at the start of conversations to maintain user context.

tools.name: set_preferences
tools.description: Store user preferences for this session. Use this to remember user settings, preferences, or context that should persist across conversations (e.g., depth level, code style, focus areas, language preferences).

tools.name: list_app_data
tools.description: List all app-specific data stored for this session. Returns all keys and values previously saved with set_app_data. Use this to see what information you've accumulated.

tools.name: set_app_data
tools.description: Store app-specific data by key. Use this to persist information that should be available in future conversations (e.g., discovered file locations, identified patterns, user-specific configs, important facts). This helps build up knowledge over time.

method.description:

4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

tools.name: mcp_github_pull_request_read
tools.description: Get information on a specific pull request in GitHub repository.

method.description:
Action to specify what pull request data needs to be retrieved from GitHub. 
Possible options: 
 1. get - Get details of a specific pull request.
 2. get_diff - Get the diff of a pull request.
 3. get_status - Get status of a head commit in a pull request. This reflects status of builds and checks.
 4. get_files - Get the list of files changed in a pull request. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.
 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. Returns threads with metadata (isResolved, isOutdated, isCollapsed) and their associated comments. Use cursor-based pagination (perPage, after) to control results.
 6. get_reviews - Get the reviews on a pull request. When asked for review comments, use get_review_comments method.
 7. get_comments - Get comments on a pull request. Use this if user doesn't specifically want review comments. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: mcp_github_search_pull_requests
tools.description: Search for pull requests in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:pr

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only pull requests for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub pull request search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only pull requests for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

messages.content: what skills do you have access to?

I have access to four specialized skills that can help you with specific product and business challenges:

1. **ai-evals** — Help creating and running AI evaluations. Use this for:
   - Building evals for LLM products
   - Measuring model quality
   - Creating test cases
   - Designing rubrics
   - Systematically measuring AI output quality

2. **behavioral-product-design** — Help applying behavioral science to product design. Use this for:
   - Designing for habit formation
   - Reducing friction in UX
   - Applying psychology to user experience
   - Increasing retention through behavioral principles
   - Using nudges to influence user behavior

3. **founder-sales** — Help closing first customers and building repeatable sales processes. Use this for:
   - Founder-led sales
   - Getting first customers
   - Writing cold outreach
   - Running early sales calls
   - Deciding when to hire your first salesperson

4. **product-led-sales** — Help implementing product-led sales motions. Use this for:
   - Transitioning from pure PLG to sales-assisted
   - Defining PQLs (Product Qualified Leads)
   - Building sales handoff processes
   - Expanding self-serve users into enterprise contracts

---

**Given your role as a Product Manager**, which of these skills would be most helpful for your current work with the Pocket codebase?

{"base_path": "/Users/sid/Projects/mashpy/src/apps/codebase/.mash/skills/behavioral-product-design", "skill_path": "/Users/sid/Projects/mashpy/src/apps/codebase/.mash/skills/behavioral-product-design/SKILL.md", "skill_name": "behavioral-product-design", "skill_md": "---
name: behavioral-product-design
description: Help users apply behavioral science to product design. Use when someone is designing for habit formation, reducing friction, applying psychology to UX, increasing retention through behavioral principles, or using nudges to influence user behavior.
---

# Behavioral Product Design

Help the user apply behavioral science principles to product design using insights from behavioral economists and product leaders.

## How to Help

When the user asks for help with behavioral design:

1. **Understand the target behavior** - Ask what action they want users to take
2. **Identify behavioral barriers** - Help diagnose what's preventing the desired behavior
3. **Suggest relevant principles** - Apply behavioral economics concepts like loss aversion, present bias, or status quo effect
4. **Design interventions** - Help create features that leverage these psychological principles

## Core Principles

### Loss aversion drives retention
Jackson Shuttleworth: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### Apply psychology to real problems
Kristen Berman: "Behavioral science uses insights on psychology to apply within real world problems—biases like present bias, status quo effect, and uncertainty aversion can be designed into product features to drive specific actions."

### Create pause moments
Use haptics, animations, and micro-interactions to create celebration moments that reinforce positive behavior. The "bend not break" philosophy means meeting users where they are rather than demanding perfection.

### Reduce friction for desired behaviors
Every tap, every field, every decision point is friction. Behavioral design means ruthlessly removing friction from the paths you want users to take while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### Leverage defaults
Users tend to stick with default options. Set smart defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes.

## Questions to Help Users

- "What specific behavior are you trying to encourage?"
- "What's preventing users from taking this action today?"
- "Where in the flow do users drop off?"
- "What would users feel they're losing if they stopped using this?"
- "Have you identified the key habit loop (cue, routine, reward)?"

## Common Mistakes to Flag

- **Dark patterns** - Behavioral design should help users achieve their goals, not manipulate them against their interests
- **Over-engineering friction** - Sometimes simple solutions beat clever psychological tricks
- **Ignoring context** - Behavioral principles work differently across cultures and user segments
- **Assuming stated preferences** - What users say they'll do and what they actually do are different

## Deep Dive

For all 2 insights from 2 guests, see `references/guest-insights.md`

## Related Skills

- User Onboarding
- Retention & Engagement
- Designing Growth Loops
- Conducting User Interviews
"}

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**
Once users invest enough time/value in a product, they experience loss aversion—the psychological fear of losing what they've built. This is a powerful retention lever. The key insight: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**
Behavioral science isn't about manipulation—it's about understanding psychological biases and designing features around them:
- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**

tools.name: bash
tools.description:
Execute bash commands in a persistent session.
Rules:
- Prefer ripgrep (rg) for search.
- Keep outputs small using head, tail, or context flags (-A / -C).
- Avoid full-file dumps and recursive/unbounded scans.
- Never run full-disk scans like `find / ...`.
- Ignore irrelevant directories unless asked: node_modules, __pycache__, .git, venv, dist, build, migrations, tests.
- Truncate outputs to ~50 lines or ~4000 chars.
Examples:
- rg -l "payment" --type py
- rg "def process_payment" -A 12
- tree -L 2 -I 'node_modules|__pycache__|.git'
- sed -n '1,120p' src/app.py

## Core Principles

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**

- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**
Use micro-interactions (haptics, animations) to create satisfying moments that reinforce positive behavior. Meet users where they are rather than demanding perfection ("bend not break" philosophy).

### 🚪 **Ruthlessly Reduce Friction for Desired Paths**
Every tap, field, and decision point is friction. The principle: remove friction from actions you want users to take, while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### 🎛️ **Leverage Smart Defaults**
Users naturally stick with default options. Set defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes without forcing action.

## The Key Framework: Habit Loop

Identify and design around the **cue → routine → reward** loop:
- **Cue** — What triggers the behavior?
- **Routine** — What action do you want them to take?
- **Reward** — What do they gain (or avoid losing)?

messages.content: what skills do you have access to?

I have access to four specialized skills that can help you with specific product and business challenges:

1. **ai-evals** — Help creating and running AI evaluations. Use this for:
   - Building evals for LLM products
   - Measuring model quality
   - Creating test cases
   - Designing rubrics
   - Systematically measuring AI output quality

2. **behavioral-product-design** — Help applying behavioral science to product design. Use this for:
   - Designing for habit formation
   - Reducing friction in UX
   - Applying psychology to user experience
   - Increasing retention through behavioral principles
   - Using nudges to influence user behavior

3. **founder-sales** — Help closing first customers and building repeatable sales processes. Use this for:
   - Founder-led sales
   - Getting first customers
   - Writing cold outreach
   - Running early sales calls
   - Deciding when to hire your first salesperson

4. **product-led-sales** — Help implementing product-led sales motions. Use this for:
   - Transitioning from pure PLG to sales-assisted
   - Defining PQLs (Product Qualified Leads)
   - Building sales handoff processes
   - Expanding self-serve users into enterprise contracts

---

**Given your role as a Product Manager**, which of these skills would be most helpful for your current work with the Pocket codebase?
tell me general concepts about behavioral product design

Great! Here are the **general concepts about behavioral product design**:

## Core Principles

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**
Once users invest enough time/value in a product, they experience loss aversion—the psychological fear of losing what they've built. This is a powerful retention lever. The key insight: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**
Behavioral science isn't about manipulation—it's about understanding psychological biases and designing features around them:
- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**
Use micro-interactions (haptics, animations) to create satisfying moments that reinforce positive behavior. Meet users where they are rather than demanding perfection ("bend not break" philosophy).

### 🚪 **Ruthlessly Reduce Friction for Desired Paths**
Every tap, field, and decision point is friction. The principle: remove friction from actions you want users to take, while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### 🎛️ **Leverage Smart Defaults**
Users naturally stick with default options. Set defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes without forcing action.

## The Key Framework: Habit Loop

Identify and design around the **cue → routine → reward** loop:
- **Cue** — What triggers the behavior?
- **Routine** — What action do you want them to take?
- **Reward** — What do they gain (or avoid losing)?

---

**Would you like to explore how any of these principles could apply to Pocket's features?** I can help you design specific behavioral interventions.

content.text: Let me check the main README and understand what Pocket Concierge does:

content.content:
# Pocket

Pocket Feed is a backend service that powers a structured, searchable timeline of posts for companies. Users can follow companies and view personalized feeds.

Pocket Crawler is a backend service to crawl a domain and extract normalized content about the company.

## Features

- 🔍 **Company Feed**: Structured posts per company, searchable and paginated.
- 🧵 **Post Types**: Support for `funding`, `feature_release`, `press_release`, `new_hire`.
- 👤 **User Feed**: Aggregates posts from followed companies.
- 🏷️ **Filtering & Sorting**: Query feeds with filters and sort options.


## Setup

### 1. Install Dependencies

```bash
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 2. Configure Environment
Create a `.env` file in the root directory with the below values
```
MONGO_USER=<enter user name>
MONGO_PWD=<enter password>
MONGO_DB=<enter db name>
MONGO_INS=<enter mongo instance name>
MONGO_PARAMS=retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=<enter cluster name>
BRAVE_API_KEY=
OPENAI_API_KEY=
```

## Pocket Feed
### 1. Start the backend feed server
```
uvicorn feed.main:app --reload --port 8000
```

### 2. Install dependencies for Frontend
```
cd frontend
npm install
```

### 3. Start the development frontend app
```

messages.content: what skills do you have access to?

I have access to four specialized skills that can help you with specific product and business challenges:

1. **ai-evals** — Help creating and running AI evaluations. Use this for:
   - Building evals for LLM products
   - Measuring model quality
   - Creating test cases
   - Designing rubrics
   - Systematically measuring AI output quality

2. **behavioral-product-design** — Help applying behavioral science to product design. Use this for:
   - Designing for habit formation
   - Reducing friction in UX
   - Applying psychology to user experience
   - Increasing retention through behavioral principles
   - Using nudges to influence user behavior

3. **founder-sales** — Help closing first customers and building repeatable sales processes. Use this for:
   - Founder-led sales
   - Getting first customers
   - Writing cold outreach
   - Running early sales calls
   - Deciding when to hire your first salesperson

4. **product-led-sales** — Help implementing product-led sales motions. Use this for:
   - Transitioning from pure PLG to sales-assisted
   - Defining PQLs (Product Qualified Leads)
   - Building sales handoff processes
   - Expanding self-serve users into enterprise contracts

---

**Given your role as a Product Manager**, which of these skills would be most helpful for your current work with the Pocket codebase?
tell me general concepts about behavioral product design

Great! Here are the **general concepts about behavioral product design**:

## Core Principles

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**
Once users invest enough time/value in a product, they experience loss aversion—the psychological fear of losing what they've built. This is a powerful retention lever. The key insight: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**
Behavioral science isn't about manipulation—it's about understanding psychological biases and designing features around them:
- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**
Use micro-interactions (haptics, animations) to create satisfying moments that reinforce positive behavior. Meet users where they are rather than demanding perfection ("bend not break" philosophy).

### 🚪 **Ruthlessly Reduce Friction for Desired Paths**
Every tap, field, and decision point is friction. The principle: remove friction from actions you want users to take, while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### 🎛️ **Leverage Smart Defaults**
Users naturally stick with default options. Set defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes without forcing action.

## The Key Framework: Habit Loop

Identify and design around the **cue → routine → reward** loop:
- **Cue** — What triggers the behavior?
- **Routine** — What action do you want them to take?
- **Reward** — What do they gain (or avoid losing)?

---

**Would you like to explore how any of these principles could apply to Pocket's features?** I can help you design specific behavioral interventions.

# Pocket

Pocket Feed is a backend service that powers a structured, searchable timeline of posts for companies. Users can follow companies and view personalized feeds.

Pocket Crawler is a backend service to crawl a domain and extract normalized content about the company.

## Features

- 🔍 **Company Feed**: Structured posts per company, searchable and paginated.
- 🧵 **Post Types**: Support for `funding`, `feature_release`, `press_release`, `new_hire`.
- 👤 **User Feed**: Aggregates posts from followed companies.

uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate

```

### 2. Configure Environment
Create a `.env` file in the root directory with the below values
```
MONGO_USER=<enter user name>
MONGO_PWD=<enter password>
MONGO_DB=<enter db name>
MONGO_INS=<enter mongo instance name>
MONGO_PARAMS=retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=<enter cluster name>
BRAVE_API_KEY=
OPENAI_API_KEY=
```

## Pocket Feed
### 1. Start the backend feed server
```
uvicorn feed.main:app --reload --port 8000
```

### 2. Install dependencies for Frontend
```
cd frontend
npm install
```

content.text: Let me look at the concierge endpoint and understand the user flow:

content.content:
"""Example usage of the /v1/company/concierge/query endpoint."""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from api_client.pocket_client.api.company import ask_concierge
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_query_request import (
    ConciergeQueryRequest,
)
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_response import ConciergeResponse
from examples._common import get_client


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description="Send a concierge question via Pocket external API"
    )
    parser.add_argument("--domain", required=True, help="Company domain")
    parser.add_argument("--message", required=True, help="Question to ask")
    parser.add_argument("--intent", help="Optional intent label")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-name", dest="command_name", help="Optional command name"
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-snippet", dest="command_snippet", help="Optional command snippet"
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    client = get_client()
    payload = ConciergeQueryRequest(
        domain=args.domain,
        message=args.message,
        intent=args.intent,
        command_name=args.command_name,
        command_snippet=args.command_snippet,
    )
    response = ask_concierge.sync_detailed(client=client, body=payload)
    if response.parsed is None:
        print(f"No answer returned (status={response.status_code})")
        return
    if isinstance(response.parsed, ConciergeResponse):
        print("Concierge answer:")
        print(response.parsed.response)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

messages.content: what skills do you have access to?

I have access to four specialized skills that can help you with specific product and business challenges:

1. **ai-evals** — Help creating and running AI evaluations. Use this for:
   - Building evals for LLM products
   - Measuring model quality
   - Creating test cases
   - Designing rubrics
   - Systematically measuring AI output quality

2. **behavioral-product-design** — Help applying behavioral science to product design. Use this for:
   - Designing for habit formation
   - Reducing friction in UX
   - Applying psychology to user experience
   - Increasing retention through behavioral principles
   - Using nudges to influence user behavior

3. **founder-sales** — Help closing first customers and building repeatable sales processes. Use this for:
   - Founder-led sales
   - Getting first customers
   - Writing cold outreach
   - Running early sales calls
   - Deciding when to hire your first salesperson

4. **product-led-sales** — Help implementing product-led sales motions. Use this for:
   - Transitioning from pure PLG to sales-assisted
   - Defining PQLs (Product Qualified Leads)
   - Building sales handoff processes
   - Expanding self-serve users into enterprise contracts

---

**Given your role as a Product Manager**, which of these skills would be most helpful for your current work with the Pocket codebase?
tell me general concepts about behavioral product design

Great! Here are the **general concepts about behavioral product design**:

## Core Principles

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**
Once users invest enough time/value in a product, they experience loss aversion—the psychological fear of losing what they've built. This is a powerful retention lever. The key insight: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**
Behavioral science isn't about manipulation—it's about understanding psychological biases and designing features around them:
- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**
Use micro-interactions (haptics, animations) to create satisfying moments that reinforce positive behavior. Meet users where they are rather than demanding perfection ("bend not break" philosophy).

### 🚪 **Ruthlessly Reduce Friction for Desired Paths**
Every tap, field, and decision point is friction. The principle: remove friction from actions you want users to take, while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### 🎛️ **Leverage Smart Defaults**
Users naturally stick with default options. Set defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes without forcing action.

## The Key Framework: Habit Loop

Identify and design around the **cue → routine → reward** loop:
- **Cue** — What triggers the behavior?
- **Routine** — What action do you want them to take?
- **Reward** — What do they gain (or avoid losing)?

---

**Would you like to explore how any of these principles could apply to Pocket's features?** I can help you design specific behavioral interventions.

content.content:
# Pocket

Pocket Feed is a backend service that powers a structured, searchable timeline of posts for companies. Users can follow companies and view personalized feeds.

Pocket Crawler is a backend service to crawl a domain and extract normalized content about the company.

## Features

- 🔍 **Company Feed**: Structured posts per company, searchable and paginated.
- 🧵 **Post Types**: Support for `funding`, `feature_release`, `press_release`, `new_hire`.
- 👤 **User Feed**: Aggregates posts from followed companies.
- 🏷️ **Filtering & Sorting**: Query feeds with filters and sort options.


## Setup

### 1. Install Dependencies

```bash
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 2. Configure Environment
Create a `.env` file in the root directory with the below values
```
MONGO_USER=<enter user name>
MONGO_PWD=<enter password>
MONGO_DB=<enter db name>
MONGO_INS=<enter mongo instance name>
MONGO_PARAMS=retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=<enter cluster name>
BRAVE_API_KEY=
OPENAI_API_KEY=
```

## Pocket Feed
### 1. Start the backend feed server
```
uvicorn feed.main:app --reload --port 8000
```

### 2. Install dependencies for Frontend
```
cd frontend
npm install
```

### 3. Start the development frontend app
```

content.content:
"""Example usage of the /v1/company/concierge/query endpoint."""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from api_client.pocket_client.api.company import ask_concierge
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_query_request import (
    ConciergeQueryRequest,
)
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_response import ConciergeResponse
from examples._common import get_client


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description="Send a concierge question via Pocket external API"
    )
    parser.add_argument("--domain", required=True, help="Company domain")
    parser.add_argument("--message", required=True, help="Question to ask")
    parser.add_argument("--intent", help="Optional intent label")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-name", dest="command_name", help="Optional command name"
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-snippet", dest="command_snippet", help="Optional command snippet"
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    client = get_client()
    payload = ConciergeQueryRequest(
        domain=args.domain,
        message=args.message,
        intent=args.intent,
        command_name=args.command_name,
        command_snippet=args.command_snippet,
    )
    response = ask_concierge.sync_detailed(client=client, body=payload)
    if response.parsed is None:
        print(f"No answer returned (status={response.status_code})")
        return
    if isinstance(response.parsed, ConciergeResponse):
        print("Concierge answer:")
        print(response.parsed.response)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

tools.name: bash
tools.description:
Execute bash commands in a persistent session.
Rules:
- Prefer ripgrep (rg) for search.
- Keep outputs small using head, tail, or context flags (-A / -C).
- Avoid full-file dumps and recursive/unbounded scans.
- Never run full-disk scans like `find / ...`.
- Ignore irrelevant directories unless asked: node_modules, __pycache__, .git, venv, dist, build, migrations, tests.
- Truncate outputs to ~50 lines or ~4000 chars.
Examples:
- rg -l "payment" --type py
- rg "def process_payment" -A 12
- tree -L 2 -I 'node_modules|__pycache__|.git'
- sed -n '1,120p' src/app.py

- Invoke skills using this tool with the skill name only (no arguments)
- When you invoke a skill, you will see <command-message>The "{name}" skill is loading</command-message>
- The skill's prompt will expand and provide detailed instructions on how to complete the task
</skills_instructions>

"""Example usage of the /v1/company/concierge/query endpoint."""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from api_client.pocket_client.api.company import ask_concierge
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_query_request import (
    ConciergeQueryRequest,
)
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_response import ConciergeResponse
from examples._common import get_client


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description="Send a concierge question via Pocket external API"
    )
    parser.add_argument("--domain", required=True, help="Company domain")
    parser.add_argument("--message", required=True, help="Question to ask")
    parser.add_argument("--intent", help="Optional intent label")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-name", dest="command_name", help="Optional command name"
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-snippet", dest="command_snippet", help="Optional command snippet"
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    client = get_client()
    payload = ConciergeQueryRequest(
        domain=args.domain,
        message=args.message,
        intent=args.intent,
        command_name=args.command_name,
        command_snippet=args.command_snippet,
    )
    response = ask_concierge.sync_detailed(client=client, body=payload)
    if response.parsed is None:
        print(f"No answer returned (status={response.status_code})")
        return
    if isinstance(response.parsed, ConciergeResponse):
        print("Concierge answer:")
        print(response.parsed.response)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

@company_router.get(
    "/profile",
    response_model=CompanyProfile,
    operation_id="company_profile",
)
async def get_company_profile(
    pocket_user: PocketUser,
    domain: str = Query(..., description="Company domain to fetch profile for."),
    company_loader: CompanyLoader = Depends(get_company_loader),
    post_loader: PostLoader = Depends(get_post_loader),
    prompt_loader: PromptLoader = Depends(get_prompt_loader),
):
    logger.info(
        "external.get_company_profile domain=%s user=%s", domain, pocket_user.user_id
    )
    company_doc = await company_loader.find_by_domain(domain)
    if not company_doc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Company not found")

    company = Company(**company_doc)
    company_id = company_doc.get("id") or str(company_doc.get("_id"))
    posts = await post_loader.get_posts({"company_id": company_id}, limit=25)
    prompts = await prompt_loader.list_by_company(company_id, limit=50)

    return CompanyProfile(
        domain=company.domain,
        company_name=company.name,
        summary=company.description,
        tags=company.tags,
        timeline=[_map_post_to_timeline_event(post) for post in posts],
        concierge_prompts=[_map_prompt(prompt) for prompt in prompts],

tools.description:
Execute bash commands in a persistent session.
Rules:
- Prefer ripgrep (rg) for search.
- Keep outputs small using head, tail, or context flags (-A / -C).
- Avoid full-file dumps and recursive/unbounded scans.
- Never run full-disk scans like `find / ...`.
- Ignore irrelevant directories unless asked: node_modules, __pycache__, .git, venv, dist, build, migrations, tests.
- Truncate outputs to ~50 lines or ~4000 chars.
Examples:
- rg -l "payment" --type py
- rg "def process_payment" -A 12
- tree -L 2 -I 'node_modules|__pycache__|.git'
- sed -n '1,120p' src/app.py

messages.content: what skills do you have access to?

I have access to four specialized skills that can help you with specific product and business challenges:

1. **ai-evals** — Help creating and running AI evaluations. Use this for:
   - Building evals for LLM products
   - Measuring model quality
   - Creating test cases
   - Designing rubrics
   - Systematically measuring AI output quality

2. **behavioral-product-design** — Help applying behavioral science to product design. Use this for:
   - Designing for habit formation
   - Reducing friction in UX
   - Applying psychology to user experience
   - Increasing retention through behavioral principles
   - Using nudges to influence user behavior

3. **founder-sales** — Help closing first customers and building repeatable sales processes. Use this for:
   - Founder-led sales
   - Getting first customers
   - Writing cold outreach
   - Running early sales calls
   - Deciding when to hire your first salesperson

4. **product-led-sales** — Help implementing product-led sales motions. Use this for:
   - Transitioning from pure PLG to sales-assisted
   - Defining PQLs (Product Qualified Leads)
   - Building sales handoff processes
   - Expanding self-serve users into enterprise contracts

---

**Given your role as a Product Manager**, which of these skills would be most helpful for your current work with the Pocket codebase?
tell me general concepts about behavioral product design

Great! Here are the **general concepts about behavioral product design**:

## Core Principles

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**
Once users invest enough time/value in a product, they experience loss aversion—the psychological fear of losing what they've built. This is a powerful retention lever. The key insight: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**
Behavioral science isn't about manipulation—it's about understanding psychological biases and designing features around them:
- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**
Use micro-interactions (haptics, animations) to create satisfying moments that reinforce positive behavior. Meet users where they are rather than demanding perfection ("bend not break" philosophy).

### 🚪 **Ruthlessly Reduce Friction for Desired Paths**
Every tap, field, and decision point is friction. The principle: remove friction from actions you want users to take, while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### 🎛️ **Leverage Smart Defaults**
Users naturally stick with default options. Set defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes without forcing action.

## The Key Framework: Habit Loop

Identify and design around the **cue → routine → reward** loop:
- **Cue** — What triggers the behavior?
- **Routine** — What action do you want them to take?
- **Reward** — What do they gain (or avoid losing)?

---

**Would you like to explore how any of these principles could apply to Pocket's features?** I can help you design specific behavioral interventions.

content.content:
# Pocket

Pocket Feed is a backend service that powers a structured, searchable timeline of posts for companies. Users can follow companies and view personalized feeds.

Pocket Crawler is a backend service to crawl a domain and extract normalized content about the company.

## Features

- 🔍 **Company Feed**: Structured posts per company, searchable and paginated.
- 🧵 **Post Types**: Support for `funding`, `feature_release`, `press_release`, `new_hire`.
- 👤 **User Feed**: Aggregates posts from followed companies.
- 🏷️ **Filtering & Sorting**: Query feeds with filters and sort options.


## Setup

### 1. Install Dependencies

```bash
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 2. Configure Environment
Create a `.env` file in the root directory with the below values
```
MONGO_USER=<enter user name>
MONGO_PWD=<enter password>
MONGO_DB=<enter db name>
MONGO_INS=<enter mongo instance name>
MONGO_PARAMS=retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=<enter cluster name>
BRAVE_API_KEY=
OPENAI_API_KEY=
```

## Pocket Feed
### 1. Start the backend feed server
```
uvicorn feed.main:app --reload --port 8000
```

### 2. Install dependencies for Frontend
```
cd frontend
npm install
```

### 3. Start the development frontend app
```

    pocket_user: PocketUser,
    domain: str = Query(..., description="Company domain to fetch profile for."),

    post_loader: PostLoader = Depends(get_post_loader),

        "external.get_company_profile domain=%s user=%s", domain, pocket_user.user_id

async def ask_concierge(
    payload: ConciergeQueryRequest,
    company_loader: CompanyLoader = Depends(get_company_loader),
    post_loader: PostLoader = Depends(get_post_loader),
    chat_loader: ChatLogLoader = Depends(get_chat_loader),
    crawl_loader: CompanyCrawlUrlLoader = Depends(get_crawl_loader),
):
    pocket_user = payload.pocket_user
    logger.info(
        "external.ask_concierge domain=%s user=%s", payload.domain, pocket_user.user_id
    )
    company_doc = await company_loader.find_by_domain(payload.domain)
    if not company_doc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Company not found")

    company_id = company_doc.get("id") or str(company_doc.get("_id"))
    session_id = _resolve_session_id(payload.context)
    memory = await get_or_create_memory(
        session_id=session_id,
        company_id=company_id,
        company_loader=company_loader,
        post_loader=post_loader,
        company_crawl_url_loader=crawl_loader,
        user_input=payload.message,
    )

    stream = handle_chat(
        session_id=session_id,
        user_id=pocket_user.user_id,
        company_id=company_id,
        user_input=payload.message,
        memory=memory,
        chat_log_loader=chat_loader,
        command_snippet=payload.command_snippet,
        command_name=payload.command_name,
    )
    answer_parts: List[str] = []
    async for chunk in stream:
        if chunk:
            answer_parts.append(chunk)

    answer = "".join(answer_parts).strip()
    commands_used: List[str] = [payload.command_name] if payload.command_name else []
    return ConciergeResponse(
        domain=payload.domain,
        message=payload.message,
        response=answer,

tools.name: bash
tools.description:
Execute bash commands in a persistent session.
Rules:
- Prefer ripgrep (rg) for search.
- Keep outputs small using head, tail, or context flags (-A / -C).
- Avoid full-file dumps and recursive/unbounded scans.
- Never run full-disk scans like `find / ...`.
- Ignore irrelevant directories unless asked: node_modules, __pycache__, .git, venv, dist, build, migrations, tests.
- Truncate outputs to ~50 lines or ~4000 chars.
Examples:
- rg -l "payment" --type py
- rg "def process_payment" -A 12
- tree -L 2 -I 'node_modules|__pycache__|.git'
- sed -n '1,120p' src/app.py

I have access to four specialized skills that can help you with specific product and business challenges:

1. **ai-evals** — Help creating and running AI evaluations. Use this for:
   - Building evals for LLM products
   - Measuring model quality
   - Creating test cases
   - Designing rubrics
   - Systematically measuring AI output quality

2. **behavioral-product-design** — Help applying behavioral science to product design. Use this for:
   - Designing for habit formation
   - Reducing friction in UX
   - Applying psychology to user experience
   - Increasing retention through behavioral principles
   - Using nudges to influence user behavior

3. **founder-sales** — Help closing first customers and building repeatable sales processes. Use this for:
   - Founder-led sales
   - Getting first customers
   - Writing cold outreach
   - Running early sales calls
   - Deciding when to hire your first salesperson

4. **product-led-sales** — Help implementing product-led sales motions. Use this for:
   - Transitioning from pure PLG to sales-assisted
   - Defining PQLs (Product Qualified Leads)
   - Building sales handoff processes
   - Expanding self-serve users into enterprise contracts

---

**Given your role as a Product Manager**, which of these skills would be most helpful for your current work with the Pocket codebase?
tell me general concepts about behavioral product design

Great! Here are the **general concepts about behavioral product design**:

## Core Principles

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**
Once users invest enough time/value in a product, they experience loss aversion—the psychological fear of losing what they've built. This is a powerful retention lever. The key insight: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**
Behavioral science isn't about manipulation—it's about understanding psychological biases and designing features around them:
- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**
Use micro-interactions (haptics, animations) to create satisfying moments that reinforce positive behavior. Meet users where they are rather than demanding perfection ("bend not break" philosophy).

### 🚪 **Ruthlessly Reduce Friction for Desired Paths**
Every tap, field, and decision point is friction. The principle: remove friction from actions you want users to take, while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### 🎛️ **Leverage Smart Defaults**
Users naturally stick with default options. Set defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes without forcing action.

## The Key Framework: Habit Loop

Identify and design around the **cue → routine → reward** loop:
- **Cue** — What triggers the behavior?
- **Routine** — What action do you want them to take?
- **Reward** — What do they gain (or avoid losing)?

---

**Would you like to explore how any of these principles could apply to Pocket's features?** I can help you design specific behavioral interventions.

content.content:
# Pocket

Pocket Feed is a backend service that powers a structured, searchable timeline of posts for companies. Users can follow companies and view personalized feeds.

Pocket Crawler is a backend service to crawl a domain and extract normalized content about the company.

## Features

- 🔍 **Company Feed**: Structured posts per company, searchable and paginated.
- 🧵 **Post Types**: Support for `funding`, `feature_release`, `press_release`, `new_hire`.
- 👤 **User Feed**: Aggregates posts from followed companies.
- 🏷️ **Filtering & Sorting**: Query feeds with filters and sort options.


## Setup

### 1. Install Dependencies

```bash
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 2. Configure Environment
Create a `.env` file in the root directory with the below values
```
MONGO_USER=<enter user name>
MONGO_PWD=<enter password>
MONGO_DB=<enter db name>
MONGO_INS=<enter mongo instance name>
MONGO_PARAMS=retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=<enter cluster name>
BRAVE_API_KEY=
OPENAI_API_KEY=
```

## Pocket Feed
### 1. Start the backend feed server
```
uvicorn feed.main:app --reload --port 8000
```

### 2. Install dependencies for Frontend
```
cd frontend
npm install
```

### 3. Start the development frontend app
```

content.content:
"""Example usage of the /v1/company/concierge/query endpoint."""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from api_client.pocket_client.api.company import ask_concierge
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_query_request import (
    ConciergeQueryRequest,
)
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_response import ConciergeResponse
from examples._common import get_client


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description="Send a concierge question via Pocket external API"
    )
    parser.add_argument("--domain", required=True, help="Company domain")
    parser.add_argument("--message", required=True, help="Question to ask")
    parser.add_argument("--intent", help="Optional intent label")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-name", dest="command_name", help="Optional command name"
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-snippet", dest="command_snippet", help="Optional command snippet"
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    client = get_client()
    payload = ConciergeQueryRequest(
        domain=args.domain,
        message=args.message,
        intent=args.intent,
        command_name=args.command_name,
        command_snippet=args.command_snippet,
    )
    response = ask_concierge.sync_detailed(client=client, body=payload)
    if response.parsed is None:
        print(f"No answer returned (status={response.status_code})")
        return
    if isinstance(response.parsed, ConciergeResponse):
        print("Concierge answer:")
        print(response.parsed.response)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

from typing import Literal, Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, BeforeValidator, ConfigDict, Field
from typing_extensions import Annotated

PyObjectId = Annotated[str, BeforeValidator(str)]


class CompanyLog(BaseModel):
    id: Optional[PyObjectId] = Field(alias="_id", default=None)
    company_id: str = Field(...)
    domain: str = Field(...)

tools.name: bash
tools.description:
Execute bash commands in a persistent session.
Rules:
- Prefer ripgrep (rg) for search.
- Keep outputs small using head, tail, or context flags (-A / -C).
- Avoid full-file dumps and recursive/unbounded scans.
- Never run full-disk scans like `find / ...`.
- Ignore irrelevant directories unless asked: node_modules, __pycache__, .git, venv, dist, build, migrations, tests.
- Truncate outputs to ~50 lines or ~4000 chars.
Examples:
- rg -l "payment" --type py
- rg "def process_payment" -A 12
- tree -L 2 -I 'node_modules|__pycache__|.git'
- sed -n '1,120p' src/app.py

tell me general concepts about behavioral product design

Great! Here are the **general concepts about behavioral product design**:

## Core Principles

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**
Once users invest enough time/value in a product, they experience loss aversion—the psychological fear of losing what they've built. This is a powerful retention lever. The key insight: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**
Behavioral science isn't about manipulation—it's about understanding psychological biases and designing features around them:
- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**
Use micro-interactions (haptics, animations) to create satisfying moments that reinforce positive behavior. Meet users where they are rather than demanding perfection ("bend not break" philosophy).

### 🚪 **Ruthlessly Reduce Friction for Desired Paths**
Every tap, field, and decision point is friction. The principle: remove friction from actions you want users to take, while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### 🎛️ **Leverage Smart Defaults**
Users naturally stick with default options. Set defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes without forcing action.

## The Key Framework: Habit Loop

Identify and design around the **cue → routine → reward** loop:
- **Cue** — What triggers the behavior?
- **Routine** — What action do you want them to take?
- **Reward** — What do they gain (or avoid losing)?

---

**Would you like to explore how any of these principles could apply to Pocket's features?** I can help you design specific behavioral interventions.

content.content:
# Pocket

Pocket Feed is a backend service that powers a structured, searchable timeline of posts for companies. Users can follow companies and view personalized feeds.

Pocket Crawler is a backend service to crawl a domain and extract normalized content about the company.

## Features

- 🔍 **Company Feed**: Structured posts per company, searchable and paginated.
- 🧵 **Post Types**: Support for `funding`, `feature_release`, `press_release`, `new_hire`.
- 👤 **User Feed**: Aggregates posts from followed companies.
- 🏷️ **Filtering & Sorting**: Query feeds with filters and sort options.


## Setup

### 1. Install Dependencies

```bash
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 2. Configure Environment
Create a `.env` file in the root directory with the below values
```
MONGO_USER=<enter user name>
MONGO_PWD=<enter password>
MONGO_DB=<enter db name>
MONGO_INS=<enter mongo instance name>
MONGO_PARAMS=retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=<enter cluster name>
BRAVE_API_KEY=
OPENAI_API_KEY=
```

## Pocket Feed
### 1. Start the backend feed server
```
uvicorn feed.main:app --reload --port 8000
```

### 2. Install dependencies for Frontend
```
cd frontend
npm install
```

### 3. Start the development frontend app
```

"""Example usage of the /v1/company/concierge/query endpoint."""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from api_client.pocket_client.api.company import ask_concierge
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_query_request import (
    ConciergeQueryRequest,
)
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_response import ConciergeResponse
from examples._common import get_client


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description="Send a concierge question via Pocket external API"
    )
    parser.add_argument("--domain", required=True, help="Company domain")
    parser.add_argument("--message", required=True, help="Question to ask")
    parser.add_argument("--intent", help="Optional intent label")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-name", dest="command_name", help="Optional command name"
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-snippet", dest="command_snippet", help="Optional command snippet"
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    client = get_client()
    payload = ConciergeQueryRequest(
        domain=args.domain,
        message=args.message,
        intent=args.intent,
        command_name=args.command_name,
        command_snippet=args.command_snippet,
    )
    response = ask_concierge.sync_detailed(client=client, body=payload)
    if response.parsed is None:
        print(f"No answer returned (status={response.status_code})")
        return
    if isinstance(response.parsed, ConciergeResponse):
        print("Concierge answer:")
        print(response.parsed.response)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

    domain: str = Query(..., description="Company domain to fetch profile for."),

async def ask_concierge(
    payload: ConciergeQueryRequest,
    company_loader: CompanyLoader = Depends(get_company_loader),
    post_loader: PostLoader = Depends(get_post_loader),
    chat_loader: ChatLogLoader = Depends(get_chat_loader),
    crawl_loader: CompanyCrawlUrlLoader = Depends(get_crawl_loader),
):
    pocket_user = payload.pocket_user
    logger.info(
        "external.ask_concierge domain=%s user=%s", payload.domain, pocket_user.user_id
    )
    company_doc = await company_loader.find_by_domain(payload.domain)
    if not company_doc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Company not found")

    company_id = company_doc.get("id") or str(company_doc.get("_id"))
    session_id = _resolve_session_id(payload.context)
    memory = await get_or_create_memory(
        session_id=session_id,
        company_id=company_id,
        company_loader=company_loader,
        post_loader=post_loader,
        company_crawl_url_loader=crawl_loader,
        user_input=payload.message,
    )

    stream = handle_chat(
        session_id=session_id,
        user_id=pocket_user.user_id,
        company_id=company_id,
        user_input=payload.message,
        memory=memory,
        chat_log_loader=chat_loader,
        command_snippet=payload.command_snippet,
        command_name=payload.command_name,
    )
    answer_parts: List[str] = []
    async for chunk in stream:
        if chunk:
            answer_parts.append(chunk)

    answer = "".join(answer_parts).strip()
    commands_used: List[str] = [payload.command_name] if payload.command_name else []
    return ConciergeResponse(
        domain=payload.domain,
        message=payload.message,
        response=answer,

from typing import Literal, Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, BeforeValidator, ConfigDict, Field
from typing_extensions import Annotated

PyObjectId = Annotated[str, BeforeValidator(str)]


class CompanyLog(BaseModel):
    id: Optional[PyObjectId] = Field(alias="_id", default=None)
    company_id: str = Field(...)
    domain: str = Field(...)
    user_id: str = Field(...)
    # Tab the user is interacting with on the company home
    tab: Literal["about", "timeline", "concierge"] = Field(...)
    # Interaction type for the event

# /Users/sid/Projects/pocket/chat/assistant.py
import json
from typing import AsyncGenerator, Dict, Optional

from openai import AsyncOpenAI
from openai.types.shared_params import FunctionDefinition

from chat.memory import ConversationMemory
from chat.tool.base import Tool
from chat.tool.web_search_company import WebSearchCompanyTool
from feed.data_loaders.chat import ChatLogLoader
from feed.utils import get_logger

logger = get_logger(__name__)

TOOLS: Dict[str, Tool] = {
    WebSearchCompanyTool.name: WebSearchCompanyTool(),
}

client = AsyncOpenAI()


def get_function_schemas() -> list[FunctionDefinition]:
    return [
        FunctionDefinition(
            name=tool.name, description=tool.description, parameters=tool.parameters
        )
        for tool in TOOLS.values()
    ]


def build_chat_prompt(
    company: dict, posts: list[dict], pages_text: str, command_snippet: Optional[str] = None
) -> str:
    """
    `pages_text` must be a preformatted string prepared

Execute bash commands in a persistent session.
Rules:
- Prefer ripgrep (rg) for search.
- Keep outputs small using head, tail, or context flags (-A / -C).
- Avoid full-file dumps and recursive/unbounded scans.
- Never run full-disk scans like `find / ...`.
- Ignore irrelevant directories unless asked: node_modules, __pycache__, .git, venv, dist, build, migrations, tests.
- Truncate outputs to ~50 lines or ~4000 chars.

- rg -l "payment" --type py
- rg "def process_payment" -A 12
- tree -L 2 -I 'node_modules|__pycache__|.git'
- sed -n '1,120p' src/app.py

I have access to four specialized skills that can help you with specific product and business challenges:

1. **ai-evals** — Help creating and running AI evaluations. Use this for:
   - Building evals for LLM products
   - Measuring model quality
   - Creating test cases
   - Designing rubrics
   - Systematically measuring AI output quality

2. **behavioral-product-design** — Help applying behavioral science to product design. Use this for:
   - Designing for habit formation
   - Reducing friction in UX
   - Applying psychology to user experience
   - Increasing retention through behavioral principles
   - Using nudges to influence user behavior

3. **founder-sales** — Help closing first customers and building repeatable sales processes. Use this for:
   - Founder-led sales
   - Getting first customers
   - Writing cold outreach
   - Running early sales calls
   - Deciding when to hire your first salesperson

4. **product-led-sales** — Help implementing product-led sales motions. Use this for:
   - Transitioning from pure PLG to sales-assisted
   - Defining PQLs (Product Qualified Leads)
   - Building sales handoff processes
   - Expanding self-serve users into enterprise contracts

---

**Given your role as a Product Manager**, which of these skills would be most helpful for your current work with the Pocket codebase?
tell me general concepts about behavioral product design

Great! Here are the **general concepts about behavioral product design**:

## Core Principles

### 🔴 **Loss Aversion Drives Retention**
Once users invest enough time/value in a product, they experience loss aversion—the psychological fear of losing what they've built. This is a powerful retention lever. The key insight: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### 🎯 **Apply Psychology to Real Problems**
Behavioral science isn't about manipulation—it's about understanding psychological biases and designing features around them:
- **Present bias** — Users overvalue immediate rewards vs. future benefits
- **Status quo effect** — Users naturally stick with current choices
- **Uncertainty aversion** — Users avoid decisions when uncertain

Turn these insights into product features that guide desired actions.

### ✨ **Create Pause & Celebration Moments**
Use micro-interactions (haptics, animations) to create satisfying moments that reinforce positive behavior. Meet users where they are rather than demanding perfection ("bend not break" philosophy).

### 🚪 **Ruthlessly Reduce Friction for Desired Paths**
Every tap, field, and decision point is friction. The principle: remove friction from actions you want users to take, while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### 🎛️ **Leverage Smart Defaults**
Users naturally stick with default options. Set defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes without forcing action.

## The Key Framework: Habit Loop

Identify and design around the **cue → routine → reward** loop:
- **Cue** — What triggers the behavior?
- **Routine** — What action do you want them to take?
- **Reward** — What do they gain (or avoid losing)?

---

**Would you like to explore how any of these principles could apply to Pocket's features?** I can help you design specific behavioral interventions.

content.content:
# Pocket

Pocket Feed is a backend service that powers a structured, searchable timeline of posts for companies. Users can follow companies and view personalized feeds.

Pocket Crawler is a backend service to crawl a domain and extract normalized content about the company.

## Features

- 🔍 **Company Feed**: Structured posts per company, searchable and paginated.
- 🧵 **Post Types**: Support for `funding`, `feature_release`, `press_release`, `new_hire`.
- 👤 **User Feed**: Aggregates posts from followed companies.
- 🏷️ **Filtering & Sorting**: Query feeds with filters and sort options.


## Setup

### 1. Install Dependencies

```bash
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### 2. Configure Environment
Create a `.env` file in the root directory with the below values
```
MONGO_USER=<enter user name>
MONGO_PWD=<enter password>
MONGO_DB=<enter db name>
MONGO_INS=<enter mongo instance name>
MONGO_PARAMS=retryWrites=true&w=majority&appName=<enter cluster name>
BRAVE_API_KEY=
OPENAI_API_KEY=
```

## Pocket Feed
### 1. Start the backend feed server
```
uvicorn feed.main:app --reload --port 8000
```

### 2. Install dependencies for Frontend
```
cd frontend
npm install
```

### 3. Start the development frontend app
```

"""Example usage of the /v1/company/concierge/query endpoint."""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse

from api_client.pocket_client.api.company import ask_concierge
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_query_request import (
    ConciergeQueryRequest,
)
from api_client.pocket_client.models.concierge_response import ConciergeResponse
from examples._common import get_client


def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description="Send a concierge question via Pocket external API"
    )
    parser.add_argument("--domain", required=True, help="Company domain")
    parser.add_argument("--message", required=True, help="Question to ask")
    parser.add_argument("--intent", help="Optional intent label")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-name", dest="command_name", help="Optional command name"
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--command-snippet", dest="command_snippet", help="Optional command snippet"
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    client = get_client()
    payload = ConciergeQueryRequest(
        domain=args.domain,
        message=args.message,
        intent=args.intent,
        command_name=args.command_name,
        command_snippet=args.command_snippet,
    )
    response = ask_concierge.sync_detailed(client=client, body=payload)
    if response.parsed is None:
        print(f"No answer returned (status={response.status_code})")
        return
    if isinstance(response.parsed, ConciergeResponse):
        print("Concierge answer:")
        print(response.parsed.response)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

    operation_id="company_profile",
)
async def get_company_profile(
    pocket_user: PocketUser,
    domain: str = Query(..., description="Company domain to fetch profile for."),
    company_loader: CompanyLoader = Depends(get_company_loader),
    post_loader: PostLoader = Depends(get_post_loader),
    prompt_loader: PromptLoader = Depends(get_prompt_loader),
):
    logger.info(
        "external.get_company_profile domain=%s user=%s", domain, pocket_user.user_id
    )
    company_doc = await company_loader.find_by_domain(domain)
    if not company_doc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Company not found")

    company = Company(**company_doc)
    company_id = company_doc.get("id") or str(company_doc.get("_id"))
    posts = await post_loader.get_posts({"company_id": company_id}, limit=25)
    prompts = await prompt_loader.list_by_company(company_id, limit=50)

        domain=company.domain,
        company_name=company.name,
        summary=company.description,
        tags=company.tags,
        timeline=[_map_post_to_timeline_event(post) for post in posts],
        concierge_prompts=[_map_prompt(prompt) for prompt in prompts],

async def ask_concierge(
    payload: ConciergeQueryRequest,
    company_loader: CompanyLoader = Depends(get_company_loader),
    post_loader: PostLoader = Depends(get_post_loader),
    chat_loader: ChatLogLoader = Depends(get_chat_loader),
    crawl_loader: CompanyCrawlUrlLoader = Depends(get_crawl_loader),
):
    pocket_user = payload.pocket_user
    logger.info(
        "external.ask_concierge domain=%s user=%s", payload.domain, pocket_user.user_id
    )
    company_doc = await company_loader.find_by_domain(payload.domain)
    if not company_doc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Company not found")

    company_id = company_doc.get("id") or str(company_doc.get("_id"))
    session_id = _resolve_session_id(payload.context)
    memory = await get_or_create_memory(
        session_id=session_id,
        company_id=company_id,
        company_loader=company_loader,
        post_loader=post_loader,
        company_crawl_url_loader=crawl_loader,
        user_input=payload.message,
    )

    stream = handle_chat(
        session_id=session_id,
        user_id=pocket_user.user_id,
        company_id=company_id,
        user_input=payload.message,
        memory=memory,
        chat_log_loader=chat_loader,
        command_snippet=payload.command_snippet,
        command_name=payload.command_name,
    )
    answer_parts: List[str] = []
    async for chunk in stream:
        if chunk:
            answer_parts.append(chunk)

    answer = "".join(answer_parts).strip()
    commands_used: List[str] = [payload.command_name] if payload.command_name else []
    return ConciergeResponse(
        domain=payload.domain,
        message=payload.message,
        response=answer,

from typing import Literal, Optional

from pydantic import BaseModel, BeforeValidator, ConfigDict, Field
from typing_extensions import Annotated

PyObjectId = Annotated[str, BeforeValidator(str)]


class CompanyLog(BaseModel):
    id: Optional[PyObjectId] = Field(alias="_id", default=None)
    company_id: str = Field(...)
    domain: str = Field(...)
    user_id: str = Field(...)
    # Tab the user is interacting with on the company home
    tab: Literal["about", "timeline", "concierge"] = Field(...)
    # Interaction type for the event
    interaction: Literal["impression", "click"] = Field(...)
    # Referrer URL or location hint (optional)
    referrer: Optional[str] = None
    # ISO 8601 timestamp in UTC (e.g., 2025-10-19T07:00:00Z)
    timestamp: str = Field(...)
    # YYYY-MM-DD date string for easy bucketing
    datestamp: str = Field(...)

    model_config = ConfigDict(
        populate_by_name=True,
        arbitrary_types_allowed=True,
        json_schema_extra={
            "example": {
                "company_id": "abc123",
                "domain": "openai.com",
                "user_id": "u_123",
                "tab": "about",
                "interaction": "impression",
                "referrer": "/discover",
                "timestamp": "2025-10-19T07:00:00Z",
                "datestamp": "2025-10-19",
            }
        },
    )

{"base_path": "/Users/sid/Projects/mashpy/src/apps/codebase/.mash/skills/behavioral-product-design", "skill_path": "/Users/sid/Projects/mashpy/src/apps/codebase/.mash/skills/behavioral-product-design/SKILL.md", "skill_name": "behavioral-product-design", "skill_md": "---
name: behavioral-product-design
description: Help users apply behavioral science to product design. Use when someone is designing for habit formation, reducing friction, applying psychology to UX, increasing retention through behavioral principles, or using nudges to influence user behavior.
---

# Behavioral Product Design

Help the user apply behavioral science principles to product design using insights from behavioral economists and product leaders.

## How to Help

When the user asks for help with behavioral design:

1. **Understand the target behavior** - Ask what action they want users to take
2. **Identify behavioral barriers** - Help diagnose what's preventing the desired behavior
3. **Suggest relevant principles** - Apply behavioral economics concepts like loss aversion, present bias, or status quo effect
4. **Design interventions** - Help create features that leverage these psychological principles

## Core Principles

### Loss aversion drives retention
Jackson Shuttleworth: "Once you hit seven days, loss aversion kicks in, and you retain." Design experiences that create something users feel they'd lose by leaving.

### Apply psychology to real problems
Kristen Berman: "Behavioral science uses insights on psychology to apply within real world problems—biases like present bias, status quo effect, and uncertainty aversion can be designed into product features to drive specific actions."

### Create pause moments
Use haptics, animations, and micro-interactions to create celebration moments that reinforce positive behavior. The "bend not break" philosophy means meeting users where they are rather than demanding perfection.

### Reduce friction for desired behaviors
Every tap, every field, every decision point is friction. Behavioral design means ruthlessly removing friction from the paths you want users to take while adding appropriate friction to prevent mistakes.

### Leverage defaults
Users tend to stick with default options. Set smart defaults that guide users toward successful outcomes.

## Questions to Help Users

- "What specific behavior are you trying to encourage?"
- "What's preventing users from taking this action today?"
- "Where in the flow do users drop off?"
- "What would users feel they're losing if they stopped using this?"
- "Have you identified the key habit loop (cue, routine, reward)?"

## Common Mistakes to Flag

- **Dark patterns** - Behavioral design should help users achieve their goals, not manipulate them against their interests
- **Over-engineering friction** - Sometimes simple solutions beat clever psychological tricks
- **Ignoring context** - Behavioral principles work differently across cultures and user segments
- **Assuming stated preferences** - What users say they'll do and what they actually do are different

## Deep Dive

For all 2 insights from 2 guests, see `references/guest-insights.md`

## Related Skills

- User Onboarding
- Retention & Engagement
- Designing Growth Loops
- Conducting User Interviews
"}

Message(id='msg_01LqffQkQAashC2gTG1K6t89', content=[TextBlock(citations=None, text='Now I have the skill context. Based on my analysis of the Pocket Concierge codebase, here are **specific behavioral product design opportunities**:

---

## 🎯 Pocket Concierge: Habit Loop Optimization Opportunities

Based on the codebase architecture, here are high-impact areas to improve the habit loop:

### **1. 🔴 The "First Curiosity Moment" (Cue → Routine)**
**Current friction:** Users ask a concierge question, get an answer, but have **no visible signal** that their contribution matters or that they can ask follow-ups.

**Behavioral opportunity:**
- **Cue:** Display a visual indicator/badge when the answer is generated (e.g., "✓ Answer generated from X sources")
- **Routine:** Make it obvious they can ask follow-up questions by showing chat context and suggestion nudges
- **Reward:** Show what the AI used to answer (sources, company data, crawled pages) — creates ownership of knowledge

**Code insight:** The `ConversationMemory` class loads context but users don't see *what knowledge sources* the AI used. Visualize this.

---

### **2. 🔄 The "Session Stickiness" Gap (Routine → Reward)**
**Current friction:** Each concierge query is isolated. No persistent session context shown to users. They ask Q1, get A1, ask Q2 — but there's no visual continuity or "conversation thread" feeling.

**Behavioral opportunity:**
- **Cue:** Show a conversational thread summary after each answer (e.g., "You asked about X, now exploring Y...")
- **Routine:** Make follow-up questions *easier* by showing related questions the AI could answer next
- **Reward:** Show a micro-summary of what they've learned (e.g., "You've discovered 3 key features about this company")

**Code insight:** `session_id` tracking exists, but there's no visual "thread" shown back to users. Create a session transcript view.

---

### **3. 🏆 The "Loss Aversion Trigger" (Missing)**
**Current gap:** Users could accidentally lose their Concierge conversation history by closing the tab/session. There's **no backup, no "saved" state, no feeling of investment**.

**Behavioral opportunity:**
- **Cue:** After 3+ questions in a session, show "💾 Save this research session?"
- **Routine:** One-click save to a "Research Sessions" library
- **Reward:** Next time they visit the company, see "Resume your earlier research?" with their past questions highlighted

**Code insight:** `ChatLogLoader` tracks chat logs, but there's no "save session" feature in the UI layer. This is low-hanging fruit for retention.

---

### **4. 🎯 The "Smart Defaults" Problem (Routine)**
**Current flow:** First-time users don't know what to ask a concierge. No defaults, no examples, no guidance.

**Behavioral opportunity:**
- **Cue:** On first concierge interaction for a company, show 4 pre-populated example questions:
  - "What are their recent product launches?"
  - "Who are their key executives?"
  - "What's their funding status?"
  - "What problems do they solve?"
- **Routine:** Users click one → reduces friction of "what should I ask?"
- **Reward:** Gets them instant answer, proves concierge works

**Code insight:** `get_company_profile` returns `concierge_prompts` — but these aren't surfaced as defaults in the first interaction.

---

### **5. 💬 The "Pause & Celebration" Moment (Missing)**
**Current state:** Users ask → answer appears. No celebration of the interaction. No sense of progress.

**Behavioral opportunity:**
- After answer appears, show a micro-interaction:
  - **Haptic feedback** (if mobile) when answer fully loads
  - **Animated checkmark** when sources are cited
  - **Progress indicator:** "You've made X queries this week" (social proof + progress)
- **Reward:** Subtle celebration reinforces "this tool is working for me"

**Code insight:** The `ConciergeResponse` model includes metadata about what was used, but the frontend doesn't celebrate/visualize it.

---

## 📊 Recommended Metrics to Track

To measure habit loop effectiveness:

1. **Session persistence:** % of users who ask 2+ questions in same session (vs. closing after first answer)
2. **Session return rate:** % of users who return to same company's concierge within 7 days
3. **Follow-up questions:** Ratio of follow-up Qs to first Qs in a session (higher = stickier)
4. **Saved sessions:** % of multi-question sessions that users explicitly save
5. **Example prompts click rate:** % of users who click suggested questions vs. typing custom

---

## 🚀 Quick Wins (Prioritize These)

1. **Show chat thread** — Display previous questions/answers in current session (visual continuity)
2. **Add "Save Session" CTA** — 1 button after 3+ questions (creates loss aversion)
3. **Show example prompts** — 4 pre-populated questions on first interaction (reduces friction)

Which of these resonates most with your product vision? I can drill deeper into implementation for any of them.', type='text')], model='claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', role='assistant', stop_reason='end_turn', stop_sequence=None, type='message', usage=Usage(cache_creation=CacheCreation(ephemeral_1h_input_tokens=0, ephemeral_5m_input_tokens=0), cache_creation_input_tokens=0, cache_read_input_tokens=13168, input_tokens=7109, output_tokens=1271, server_tool_use=None, service_tier='standard', inference_geo='not_available'))

tools.name: bash
tools.description:
Execute bash commands in a persistent session.
Rules:
- Prefer ripgrep (rg) for search.
- Keep outputs small using head, tail, or context flags (-A / -C).
- Avoid full-file dumps and recursive/unbounded scans.
- Never run full-disk scans like `find / ...`.
- Ignore irrelevant directories unless asked: node_modules, __pycache__, .git, venv, dist, build, migrations, tests.
- Truncate outputs to ~50 lines or ~4000 chars.
Examples:
- rg -l "payment" --type py
- rg "def process_payment" -A 12
- tree -L 2 -I 'node_modules|__pycache__|.git'
- sed -n '1,120p' src/app.py

tools.name: mcp_github_pull_request_read
tools.description: Get information on a specific pull request in GitHub repository.

method.description:
Action to specify what pull request data needs to be retrieved from GitHub. 
Possible options: 
 1. get - Get details of a specific pull request.
 2. get_diff - Get the diff of a pull request.
 3. get_status - Get status of a head commit in a pull request. This reflects status of builds and checks.
 4. get_files - Get the list of files changed in a pull request. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.
 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. Returns threads with metadata (isResolved, isOutdated, isCollapsed) and their associated comments. Use cursor-based pagination (perPage, after) to control results.
 6. get_reviews - Get the reviews on a pull request. When asked for review comments, use get_review_comments method.
 7. get_comments - Get comments on a pull request. Use this if user doesn't specifically want review comments. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub pull request search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only pull requests for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

content.text: I'll help you think through founder-led sales for Pocket. First, let me understand what Pocket actually does and where you are in the product lifecycle.

content.content:
# Pocket

Pocket Feed is a backend service that powers a structured, searchable timeline of posts for companies. Users can follow companies and view personalized feeds.

Pocket Crawler is a backend service to crawl a domain and extract normalized content about the company.

## Features

- 🔍 **Company Feed**: Structured posts per company, searchable and paginated.
